stem: Use Gaussian elimination on \( \begin{pmatrix} [[a1]] & [[b1]] & | & [[c1]] \\ [[a2]] & [[b2]] & | & [[c2]] \end{pmatrix} \) to solve \( ([[a1]])x + ([[b1]])y = [[c1]] \), \( ([[a2]])x + ([[b2]])y = [[c2]] \). What is \( x \)?
solution: Eliminating \( y \) (multiply row 1 by \( [[b2]] \), row 2 by \( [[b1]] \), and subtract) isolates \( x = [[p]] \) (with \( y = [[q]] \)), so \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the value of \( y \), not \( x \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign for \( x \).
This adds the two unknowns instead of reporting \( x \) alone.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( x \).
This is off by one from the correct value of \( x \).
This is off by two from the correct value of \( x \).

solution: Elimination reduces the system to a unique solution \( x=[[p]] \), \( y=[[q]] \), \( z=[[r]] \) (the coefficient determinant is nonzero), so \( z = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the value of \( x \), not \( z \).
This is the value of \( y \), not \( z \).
This mixes up two of the other unknowns instead of reporting \( z \).
This is off by one from the correct value of \( z \).
This is off by one from the correct value of \( z \).
This is off by two from the correct value of \( z \).

stem: After forward elimination on the coefficient matrix \( \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[r31]] & [[r32]] & [[r33]] \end{pmatrix} \) of a \( 3\times3 \) linear system, how many pivots (i.e. what is the rank) does it produce?
solution: Row 3 is the sum of rows 1 and 2, so it is eliminated to an all-zero row with no new pivot, while rows 1 and 2 remain independent (their leading \( 2\times2 \) minor \( ([[a]])([[e]])-([[b]])([[d]]) \ne 0 \)). Elimination therefore produces [[answer]] pivots.
distractor_rationales: This counts all three rows as independent, missing that row 3 reduces to zero.
This under-counts the independent rows; TWO rows survive elimination, not one.
A matrix with a nonzero row cannot reduce to zero pivots.
This over-counts the number of pivots produced by elimination.
This under-counts the number of pivots produced by elimination.

stem: A vendor sells burgers for \( p \) dollars and fries for \( q \) dollars. One order of \( [[a1]] \) burgers and \( [[b1]] \) fries costs \( [[c1]] \) dollars; another order of \( [[a2]] \) burgers and \( [[b2]] \) fries costs \( [[c2]] \) dollars. Setting up and eliminating one variable from this linear system, what is the price \( q \) of an order of fries, in dollars?
solution: Eliminating \( p \) from the two order equations and solving gives \( p=[[p]] \) and \( q=[[q]] \), so the price of fries is \( q = [[answer]] \) dollars.
distractor_rationales: This is the price of a burger, not fries.
This lumps both orders' totals together instead of solving the system properly.
This ignores the burgers entirely when isolating the fry price from just one order.
This is off by one from the correct price.
This is off by one from the correct price.
This is off by two from the correct price.
